title: Hetero Diels-Alder Cycloaddition – Importance and Mechanism
family_names: Hetero Diels-Alder Cycloaddition|Hetero D-A Cycloaddition|HDA Reaction
summary: Overview page for the hetero Diels-Alder cycloaddition describing [4+2] or related cycloadditions in which one atom of the diene or dienophile is a heteroatom, including normal and inverse electron-demand variants.
notes: Key concepts: heterodienes and heterodienophiles, normal versus inverse electron-demand activation, concerted or stepwise pathways, Lewis-acid catalysis, and regio- and stereochemical control in oxygen- and nitrogen-containing cycloadduct formation.

title: Hetero Diels-Alder Cycloaddition – Synthetic Applications
family_names: Hetero Diels-Alder Cycloaddition|Hetero D-A Cycloaddition|HDA Reaction
summary: Application page for the hetero Diels-Alder cycloaddition featuring reveromycin B, strychnine, lepadin A, and the Taxol A-ring side chain assembled through normal or inverse electron-demand hetero cycloadditions.
notes: Examples emphasize biomimetic annulations, acylnitroso or iminium-derived heterodienes, intramolecular HDA manifolds, and efficient installation of densely functionalized oxygen- and nitrogen-heterocycles.

title: Hofmann Elimination – Importance and Mechanism
summary: Overview page for the Hofmann elimination describing exhaustive methylation of amines to quaternary ammonium salts followed by base-promoted beta-elimination to furnish the least substituted alkene.
notes: Key concepts: quaternary ammonium hydroxides, anti elimination, Hofmann versus Saytzeff selectivity, pyrolytic decomposition, and degradation of alkaloid-derived amines to define unsaturation patterns.

title: Hofmann Elimination – Synthetic Applications
summary: Application page for the Hofmann elimination featuring 4-demethoxydaunomycin, cryptosporin, atisine, and picrasin where exhaustive methylation and elimination reveal key allylic or exocyclic olefins.
notes: Examples emphasize strategic deamination, spontaneous elimination of quaternary ammonium salts, alkaloid degradations, and conversion of dimethylamino substituents into alkenes in complex settings.

title: Hofmann-Löffler-Freytag Reaction – Importance and Mechanism
summary: Overview page for the Hofmann-Loffler-Freytag reaction describing radical-mediated remote C-H amination of N-haloamines or related precursors via 1,5-hydrogen abstraction and cyclization to saturated nitrogen heterocycles.
notes: Key concepts: N-centered radicals, 1,5-hydrogen abstraction, halogen rebound, cyclization under acidic or neutral photochemical conditions, and Suarez-type remote functionalization variants.

title: Hofmann-Löffler-Freytag Reaction – Synthetic Applications
summary: Application page for the Hofmann-Loffler-Freytag reaction featuring podocarpane alkaloid cores, bicyclic aza- and oxa-systems, and saccharin derivatives assembled through remote radical amination or Suarez-type oxidative cyclization.
notes: Examples emphasize bridge-forming radical cyclizations, carbohydrate-derived intramolecular C-H functionalization, and N-radical mediated remote oxidation or glycosidation.

title: Hofmann Rearrangement – Importance and Mechanism
summary: Overview page for the Hofmann rearrangement describing conversion of primary carboxamides into amines or trapped isocyanate derivatives through N-haloamide formation and migration to an isocyanate.
notes: Key concepts: N-haloamide intermediates, concerted rearrangement to isocyanates, retention of configuration, hydrolysis to one-carbon-shorter amines, and trapping of isocyanates with alcohols or amines.

title: Hofmann Rearrangement – Synthetic Applications
summary: Application page for the Hofmann rearrangement featuring epibatidine, cepharamine, preussin, and capreomycin intermediates where chemoselective amide contraction installs amines or carbamates late in synthesis.
notes: Examples emphasize hypervalent iodine or classical hypobromite variants, stereoretentive migration, and selective conversion of primary amides into protected amines in complex natural product synthesis.

title: Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons Olefination – Importance and Mechanism
summary: Overview page for the Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons olefination describing phosphonate-stabilized carbanion addition to aldehydes or ketones followed by elimination to form alkenes, typically with high E selectivity.
notes: Key concepts: phosphonate carbanions, HWE versus Wittig reactivity, E/Z selectivity control, Still-Gennari-type modifications, mild base conditions, and water-soluble phosphate byproducts facilitating product isolation.

title: Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons Olefination – Synthetic Applications
summary: Application page for the Horner-Wadsworth-Emmons olefination featuring cylindrocyclophane A, carbacephalosporin, and callipeltoside A assembled through intramolecular or intermolecular phosphonate olefination steps.
notes: Examples emphasize macrocyclizing HWE dimerization, beta-lactam-containing olefinations, and late-stage coupling of complex side chains to phosphonate- or phosphine-oxide-derived fragments.
